Press enter or click to view image in full size

When building a large Angular application inside an Nx monorepo, it’s common to organize reusable code into shared libraries.
It keeps the codebase clean.
It improves maintainability.
It encourages reuse.
But there’s one hidden performance issue that many developers don’t notice until they inspect the production bundle.
You import a single utility function
and somehow your initial JavaScript bundle becomes significantly larger.
Let’s understand why this happens and how to fix it properly.
The Problem
Imagine you have a shared utility library.
libs/common/helpersInside it:
libs/
└── common/
└── helpers/
├── date.helper.ts
├── currency.helper.ts
├── string.helper.ts
├── validator.helper.ts
└── index.tsThe barrel file exports everything.
// index.tsexport * from './date.helper';
export * from './currency.helper';
export * from './string.helper';
export * from './validator.helper';
